title: Power BI - Data Modeling for Security Snapshot Data: Star Schema, Unpivoting, and Period Combination
description: Production modeling patterns for periodic security snapshot data: star schema design, unpivoting wide CSV tables to tall fact tables in Power Query, combining monthly and quarterly snapshots in one model, and the 'this month vs change' pattern

star_schema_for_security_snapshots.overview: A star schema for security snapshot reporting has one central fact table (SecuritySnapshots) and two small dimension tables (DateTable, MetricDim). All relationships radiate from the fact table to dimensions.

columns: SnapshotDate  — Date, first day of the period (2026-01-01, 2026-02-01…)
MetricID      — Integer foreign key to MetricDim
Value         — Integer or decimal, the count or score for this metric and period
PeriodType    — Text: 'Monthly' or 'Quarterly' (used when both granularities coexist)
SourceFile    — Text: original filename, useful for troubleshooting data gaps
YearMonthKey  — Integer YYYYMM, e.g. 202601 (join key to DateTable)
fact_table_design.grain: One row per period per metric. Never store MoM delta or % change as columns in the fact table — compute these in DAX measures. Storing pre-computed deltas causes double-counting and makes time intelligence impossible.

columns: SortOrder       — Integer for row ordering in matrix
RedThreshold    — Integer: value at which this metric is red
AmberThreshold  — Integer: value at which this metric is amber
LowIsGood       — Boolean: TRUE for counts (lower = better), FALSE for scores (higher = better)

star_schema_for_security_snapshots.relationships:
DateTable[YearMonthKey] (1) → SecuritySnapshots[YearMonthKey] (*) — single direction, DateTable filters SecuritySnapshots
MetricDim[MetricID] (1) → SecuritySnapshots[MetricID] (*) — single direction, MetricDim filters SecuritySnapshots

Do NOT create relationships between DateTable and MetricDim — they are independent dimensions.
star_schema_for_security_snapshots.why_not_wide_format: A wide fact table (one column per metric: Sev5Count, Sev4Count, ExternalCount…) cannot be used with a MetricDim dimension. You cannot filter by metric name, sort by SortOrder, or apply per-metric thresholds. The tall format (one row per metric per period) is always preferred for flexible, reusable DAX measures.

unpivoting_wide_csv_to_tall.problem:
Logic App CSVs often export wide format: one row per period, one column per metric:
Date       | Sev5 | Sev4 | External | Internal | Alerts
2026-01-01 |  142 |  387 |       28 |       74 |     15

This must be unpivoted to tall format before loading.
power_query_unpivot_steps: 1. In Power Query, after loading and promoting headers, select all columns you want to KEEP fixed (e.g. Date, Region — these are NOT metrics)
2. Right-click the selected fixed columns > Unpivot Other Columns
3. Power Query creates two new columns: 'Attribute' (metric name) and 'Value' (the count)
5. Change 'Count' column type to Int64.Type or Decimal.Type
unpivoting_wide_csv_to_tall.unpivot_other_columns_explanation: Table.UnpivotOtherColumns(table, pivot_columns, attribute_column_name, value_column_name): keeps pivot_columns fixed, and for every other column creates one row per column with the column name in attribute_column_name and the cell value in value_column_name. This is the correct function when you know which columns to keep fixed.

this_month_vs_change_model.overview: Never store MoM change as a column in the fact table. Calculate it in DAX from the raw values. The fact table contains only the raw snapshot value for each period.
this_month_vs_change_model.why: Storing computed deltas creates a circular dependency: the delta for March depends on February's value, which means you cannot modify February's data without recalculating all downstream deltas. DAX time intelligence handles this correctly and dynamically based on whatever filter context the visual applies.
this_month_vs_change_model.measure_pattern:
// The complete measure set for one metric:
// (With MetricDim[MetricName] on matrix rows, these work for ALL metrics)

Metric Value =
    SUM(SecuritySnapshots[Value])

Metric PM =
    CALCULATE([Metric Value], PREVIOUSMONTH('DateTable'[Date]))

Metric MoM Change =
    VAR _C = [Metric Value]
    VAR _P = [Metric PM]
    RETURN IF(NOT ISBLANK(_C) && NOT ISBLANK(_P), _C - _P, BLANK())

Metric MoM % =
    VAR _C = [Metric Value]
    VAR _P = [Metric PM]
    RETURN IF(ISBLANK(_P) || _P = 0, BLANK(), DIVIDE(_C - _P, ABS(_P)))

Metric LY =
    CALCULATE([Metric Value], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('DateTable'[Date]))

Metric YoY % =
    VAR _C = [Metric Value]
    VAR _L = [Metric LY]
    RETURN IF(ISBLANK(_L) || _L = 0, BLANK(), DIVIDE(_C - _L, ABS(_L)))

combining_monthly_and_quarterly_snapshot.scenario: You have two CSVs in SharePoint: monthly snapshots (fine-grained, all metrics) and quarterly snapshots (aggregated, including metrics not in monthly CSVs, e.g. quarter-end security scores).

approach_single_fact_table.description: Load both into the same SecuritySnapshots fact table with a PeriodType column. Use the PeriodType in measures to filter appropriately.
approach_single_fact_table.date_key: Both monthly and quarterly snapshots join to the same DateTable via YearMonthKey. Quarterly snapshots use the first month of the quarter: Q1 2026 = 202601, Q2 2026 = 202604.

approach_separate_fact_tables.description: Keep monthly and quarterly snapshots in separate fact tables (MonthlySnapshots and QuarterlySnapshots). Both join to the same DateTable.
approach_separate_fact_tables.when_to_use: Prefer this approach when quarterly snapshots contain completely different metrics from monthly snapshots, or when quarterly data has different schema.
approach_separate_fact_tables.relationship_setup:
DateTable[YearMonthKey] (1) → MonthlySnapshots[YearMonthKey] (*)
DateTable[YearMonthKey] (1) → QuarterlySnapshots[YearMonthKey] (*)

The DateTable is reused for both. When user filters to Q1 2026 (months Jan, Feb, Mar), both fact tables are filtered to those months. Quarterly fact table typically only has data for month=start of quarter.
approach_separate_fact_tables.measure_scoping: Monthly measures reference MonthlySnapshots, quarterly measures reference QuarterlySnapshots. Put them in the appropriate fact table in the Modeling pane.

combining_monthly_and_quarterly_snapshot.quarterly_aggregation_from_monthly:
// If you only have monthly data but want quarterly aggregates:
Quarterly Count =
    CALCULATE(
        [Metric Value],
        DATESQTD('DateTable'[Date])  -- quarter-to-date
    )
// Or for a full quarter total when viewing by quarter:
Quarterly Total =
    CALCULATE(
        [Metric Value],
        DATESINPERIOD(
            'DateTable'[Date],
            ENDOFQUARTER('DateTable'[Date]),
            -1,
            QUARTER
        )
    )

excel_files_vs_csv.excel_as_source: Logic Apps can write to Excel files in SharePoint instead of CSVs. Power BI connects to Excel with SharePoint.Files() + Excel.Workbook() instead of Csv.Document(). The M query structure is almost identical.
excel_files_vs_csv.excel_vs_csv_for_logic_apps: CSVs are simpler to generate from Logic Apps (just string concatenation or a JSON-to-CSV action) and are smaller in storage. Excel files are larger and require the Excel Online connector in Logic Apps, but support multiple named tables per file and preserve data types without manual parsing. For automated pipelines, CSVs are generally preferred.

common_mistakes: Storing pre-computed MoM deltas in the fact table — impossible to recompute if historical data changes, and breaks DAX time intelligence.
Wide format fact table (columns = metrics) — cannot be used with a metric dimension, makes adding new metrics require schema changes.
Not normalising SnapshotDate to the first of the month — dates like 2026-03-15 won't join correctly to DateTable where the expected match is 2026-03-01.
Loading quarterly and monthly snapshots without a PeriodType column — impossible to distinguish them in DAX, measures double-count.

Using Table.Unpivot on fixed metric column names when new metrics are added over time — the query breaks when a new metric column appears in newer CSVs. Use Table.UnpivotOtherColumns instead to pivot everything except the fixed identifier columns.
MetricDim as an in-memory Power Query table without a relationship to the fact table — dimensions must be in the data model with a relationship, not just loaded as loose tables.
Combining monthly and quarterly snapshots without aligning the YearMonthKey — quarterly Q2 2026 must use 202604 (April), not 202606 (June end).
